## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)

The conflict between the United States and Israel against Iran intensified over the past 24-48 hours, marking a month since its commencement. US and Israeli forces continued high-tempo strikes against Iranian missile infrastructure, naval assets, and military production facilities, including nuclear sites at Ardakan and Arak. In retaliation, Iran launched ballistic missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Prince Sultan air base, injuring at least 12 US service members and damaging aircraft. The Iranian-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen also entered the conflict, firing a missile at Israel which was reportedly intercepted.

Amid these tensions, the G7 Foreign Ministers' Summit took place in France on March 26-27, with India participating as a partner country. Discussions focused on the Middle East war, global energy, food, and fuel security, and the urgent need for global governance reforms. Russia announced a ban on gasoline exports starting April 1, citing turmoil in global oil and oil product markets caused by the Middle East crisis. Global economic markets remain on edge due to uncertainty surrounding energy flows through the Persian Gulf, contributing to volatility in financial markets.

---

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)

### Americas
In the United States, negotiations continued regarding a Republican-backed bill to fund the Department of Homeland Security amid an ongoing shutdown affecting several agencies. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agents were deployed to 14 airports earlier this week as approximately 50,000 Transportation Security Administration (TSA) officers worked without pay due to the funding lapse. Extreme heat events shattered March temperature records across the United States.

### Europe
The European Union imposed sanctions on two Chinese companies and one Iranian firm, along with two individuals, for their involvement in cyberattacks targeting EU member states. An outbreak of invasive meningococcal disease has been reported in Kent, England, with 22 cases and two deaths, alongside one confirmed case in France. Ukraine and Saudi Arabia concluded a defense cooperation agreement on March 27, with Ukraine set to assist Saudi Arabia with air defenses.

### Asia-Pacific
The Boao Forum for Asia (BFA) Annual Conference 2026 concluded in Hainan Province, China, with the Global South emerging as a more active force in shaping global economic governance. Pakistan's Senate observed Earth Hour on March 28, calling for collective action to address climate change and promote environmental sustainability, noting the country's vulnerability to extreme weather events. Extreme heat also smashed records in Australia.

### Africa
Sudan is experiencing one of the world's worst humanitarian catastrophes due to ongoing conflict, with millions displaced and famine conditions exacerbated. Reports highlight documented atrocities, including widespread sexual violence and deliberate targeting of ethnic communities. A large new study estimated that over a quarter of a million people worldwide die from meningitis annually, with children accounting for a third of these deaths, many occurring in Africa.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region

The US-Israeli air campaign against Iran continued, with strikes targeting Iranian nuclear infrastructure, including the Arak Heavy Water Production Facility and the Ardakan Yellowcake Production Plant. Industrial production facilities across Iran were also reportedly hit, including steel and cement factories. Iran retaliated with ballistic missile and drone attacks on Saudi Arabia's Prince Sultan air base, injuring US personnel.

The Iranian-backed Houthi militants in Yemen launched a missile towards Israel, marking their entry into the conflict. Attacks were also reported at Kuwait's airport and a port in Oman. Diplomatic activity increased, with competing ceasefire proposals from Washington and Tehran reflecting widening gaps over acceptable end-state conditions. The British foreign secretary condemned Iran's attacks and its blocking of the Strait of Hormuz, stating Tehran cannot hold the global economy hostage.

## SECTION 4 — Technology and the Future

Recent advancements in Artificial Intelligence have shifted focus towards "Agentic AI," which are autonomous, goal-oriented digital coworkers capable of understanding complex objectives and executing multi-step workflows. Lucid Software has updated its AI-driven work acceleration tools, including a new Process Agent for streamlining diagram and documentation creation. Google is globally rolling out Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, a conversational audio AI integrated into search and developer tools. Anthropic also expanded AI agent access with messaging-based Claude Code Channels, allowing asynchronous workflows.

In space exploration, NASA announced major realignments, cancelling the planned Lunar Gateway and instead committing to a permanent base on the Moon's surface. The first human Moon landing since 1972 is anticipated in 2028, with robotic missions starting in 2027 and a full-time lunar base operational by 2036. The Artemis II mission, set to launch around April 1, 2026, will send four astronauts on a 10-day voyage around the Moon, marking the first time humans have left Earth orbit since Apollo 17. NASA also announced the Space Reactor-1 (SR-1) Freedom mission, a nuclear-electric powered rocket aiming for Mars in 2028.

## SECTION 5 — Tech Industry & Global Business Shifts

The cybersecurity landscape experienced a significant influx of attacks in March 2026, with ransomware and malware incidents carried out by threat actors linked to North Korea, Russia, and Iran. Cyber incidents remain the top global business risk for the fifth consecutive year, with AI rapidly rising to the second position in 2026, highlighting emerging risks across industry sectors. A major cyberattack hit medical technology company Stryker in March 2026, linked to an Iran-aligned hacktivist group, resulting in a mass wipe of company computers.

Other notable cybersecurity incidents include a Match Group data breach, exposing 10 million user records, and ransomware attacks by the Medusa gang targeting the University of Mississippi Medical Center and Passaic County, New Jersey. Financial frauds and digital scams exploiting fear and trust have also surged, including a "digital arrest" scam in Maharashtra, India, and a ₹160 crore embezzlement from the Panchkula Municipal Corporation's fixed deposits. The EU has sanctioned Chinese and Iranian companies for their involvement in cyberattacks targeting member states.

## SECTION 6 — Science & Research Breakthroughs

A significant new study estimated that over a quarter of a million people globally die from meningitis annually, with a third of these deaths occurring in children, particularly in Africa's meningitis belt. This research, drawing on figures from the Global Burden of Disease study, described itself as the most comprehensive global assessment of meningitis to date. In climate science, vital Arctic sea ice reached its lowest measured winter level as unprecedented heat records were shattered across continents. This indicates ongoing impacts of a warming Earth, with concerns raised about intensifying extreme weather events.

## SECTION 7 — Global Health & Biosecurity Watch

A newly identified SARS-CoV-2 variant, BA32, is garnering attention from global public health officials due to its spread across multiple continents and ability to evade immune defenses. First detected in South Africa in November 2024, BA32 has been found in at least 23 countries. Measles cases continue to rise globally, with 1,575 confirmed cases reported in the United States as of March 26, 2026, by 32 jurisdictions.

An outbreak of invasive meningococcal disease has been reported in Kent, England, affecting 22 individuals and causing two deaths, with one confirmed case in France. Globally, over 500,000 cases of dengue and more than 100 dengue-related deaths have been reported since the beginning of 2026, a decrease compared to the same period in 2025. Chikungunya virus disease has seen 32,758 cases and nine deaths detected from 18 countries (excluding EU/EEA) since the start of 2026.

The ongoing US-Israel war with Iran has significantly impacted global security, with Iran launching ballistic missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia, injuring US service members. Iranian-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen also engaged by firing a missile at Israel. Cyber warfare remains a prominent threat, with a large cyberattack on medical technology company Stryker linked to an Iran-aligned hacktivist group. Ransomware attacks continue to pose significant risks, with the Medusa gang claiming high-profile attacks on the University of Mississippi Medical Center and Passaic County, New Jersey. Geopolitical confrontation and state-based armed conflict are ranked as the top global risks for 2026, according to the World Economic Forum, highlighting an age of competition where multilateral mechanisms are under strain. The militarization of US cities and the dissolution of US soft power are also noted as destabilizing factors.

## SECTION 9 — Energy, Climate & Environment Watch

Vital Arctic sea ice has shrunk to tie its lowest measured level for the winter season, as unprecedented extreme heat events shattered March temperature records across the United States, Mexico, Australia, Northern Africa, and parts of Northern Europe. Red Flag Warnings were issued across parts of the US, from the Plains to the Southeast, due to critical fire weather conditions. Pakistan's Senate observed Earth Hour, highlighting the country's acute vulnerability to climate change impacts, including accelerating glacial melt and intensifying water stress.

In energy markets, Russia announced a ban on gasoline exports from April 1 to July 31, citing turmoil in global oil and oil product markets. Brent crude oil prices have seen a significant increase of approximately 50% from last month, reaching $113.39 a barrel on March 27, partly due to uncertainty regarding energy flows through the Persian Gulf. Despite federal rollbacks, over 20 US states with clean electricity or net-zero targets are advancing climate policies, and the UK has accelerated renewable energy auctions in response to the Middle East crisis. The US EPA has extended the reporting deadline for the 2025 annual greenhouse gas report from March 31 to October 30, 2026.

## SECTION 10 — Global Economy & Market Signals

**Crude Oil:** Brent crude oil price on Friday, March 27, was $113.39 a barrel, reflecting about a 50 percent increase from last month. Oil prices edged higher, supported by ongoing geopolitical tensions and a reported drawdown in U.S. crude inventories.
**Equities:** Major global asset classes experienced a divergent session on Thursday, with U.S. equities pulling back from recent highs, driven by a recalibration of interest rate expectations. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite retreated from record levels, with a broad-based sell-off led by technology and growth-oriented sectors.
**Currency:** The U.S. Dollar Index was relatively muted, trading within a narrow range, finding modest support from hawkish-leaning Federal Reserve rhetoric.
**Central Banks:** Commentary from Federal Reserve officials emphasized patience regarding potential interest rate cuts, pushing market-implied expectations for the first rate cut further into the second half of the year. This recalibration influenced risk assets that had rallied aggressively on earlier optimism.

## SECTION 13 — India National (Politics & Governance)

External Affairs Minister S. Jaishankar met French President Emmanuel Macron on the sidelines of the G7 Foreign Ministers' Summit in France on Friday, March 27, conveying greetings from Prime Minister Narendra Modi. During the summit, Jaishankar flagged the concerns of Global South nations over energy, food, and fuel security, emphasizing the urgent need for global governance reforms and resilient trade corridors. He also held separate conversations with counterparts from the U.S., Saudi Arabia, Germany, Canada, South Korea, and Ukraine, where the West Asia crisis was a prominent topic.

## SECTION 14 — Telangana State Updates

The Telangana Legislative Assembly has approved a resolution for the state government to take over Phase-I of the Hyderabad Metro Rail project from Larsen & Toubro (L&T). Chief Minister A. Revanth Reddy stated that this decision aims to resolve technical, legal, and administrative issues hindering further expansion of the Metro network, with proposals for Phase II (A) and Phase II (B) already submitted to the Centre. Telangana Chief Minister A. Revanth Reddy launched the "Toli Mudda" breakfast scheme for Anganwadi children and pledged to ensure all Anganwadi centers have permanent buildings within three years, addressing pending issues for Anganwadi staff. Chief Minister Revanth Reddy unveiled the "Bharat Future City" plan, which includes a dedicated AI hub in Hyderabad, to tackle challenges posed by artificial intelligence. He also proposed a "People Credits" system for AI companies to ensure they contribute to employment generation or workforce development.

---

## SECTION 15 — Andhra Pradesh State Updates

The Andhra Pradesh Assembly unanimously passed a resolution requesting the Central Government to grant legal sanctity and legitimacy to Amaravati as the sole capital of the state. Chief Minister N. Chandrababu Naidu moved the resolution, seeking amendments to Section 5 of the Andhra Pradesh Reorganisation Act, 2014. A tragic bus fire in Andhra Pradesh resulted in 13 deaths, with President Murmu expressing condolences and Prime Minister announcing ex-gratia payments. AM/NS India commenced work on a significant ₹1.36 trillion steel plant project in Andhra Pradesh.

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News

### Escalation of US-Iran Conflict and Regional Spillover

**What Happened:** The conflict between the United States and Israel against Iran significantly escalated over the past 24-48 hours. US and Israeli forces continued extensive strikes on Iranian military and nuclear infrastructure, including heavy water production and yellowcake facilities, and industrial targets across Iran. In retaliation, Iran launched ballistic missiles and drones at Saudi Arabia's Prince Sultan air base, resulting in injuries to at least 12 US service members and damage to aircraft. Concurrently, the Iranian-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen expanded their involvement by launching a missile at Israel, which was intercepted.

**Root Causes:** The conflict stems from deep-seated geopolitical tensions, including concerns over Iran's nuclear program, its regional influence through proxy groups, and the strategic importance of the Strait of Hormuz. US and Israeli strategic objectives aim to degrade Iran's missile and drone production capabilities and to curb its ability to threaten regional stability. Iran, in turn, seeks to retaliate against US and Israeli aggression and leverage its strategic position to exert pressure on global energy markets.

**Historical Background:** The current escalation builds upon a history of adversarial relations between the US, Israel, and Iran, punctuated by periods of proxy conflicts, sanctions, and covert operations. Past Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear infrastructure and recurring tensions in the Persian Gulf have contributed to a cycle of escalation. The US presence in the region and its alliances with Gulf states have long been a point of contention for Iran.

**Key Actors:** The primary actors are the United States and Israel, conducting coordinated military operations. On the opposing side, Iran and its allied "Axis of Resistance" including the Houthi rebels in Yemen are involved. Saudi Arabia's Prince Sultan air base was a direct target, making it a crucial location in the immediate impact. International bodies like the G7 and UN are engaged in diplomatic efforts and addressing humanitarian consequences.

**Strategic Importance:** The escalation carries significant strategic importance due to its direct impact on regional stability, global energy markets, and international trade routes, particularly the Strait of Hormuz. The involvement of various actors and proxy groups increases the risk of a broader regional conflict. The targeting of nuclear facilities also raises concerns about proliferation and the future of Iran's nuclear program.

**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** Continued escalation could lead to prolonged regional instability, further disruption of global energy supplies, and a potential humanitarian crisis. The conflict may hasten a re-evaluation of international diplomatic frameworks and alliances in the Middle East. The degradation of Iran's military capabilities could alter the balance of power, while sustained Iranian resistance might entrench hardline elements within its leadership. The economic repercussions, including sustained high oil prices, could impact global growth and inflation for an extended period.

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)

- On March 28, 1854, during the Crimean War, Britain and France declared war on Russia.
- In 1910, the first seaplane took off from a water runway, piloted by Henri Fabre.
- On March 28, 1939, the Spanish Civil War neared its conclusion as Madrid fell to the forces of Generalissimo Francisco Franco after a three-year siege.
- In 1979, America's worst commercial nuclear accident occurred at the Three Mile Island nuclear power plant in Pennsylvania, involving a partial meltdown and radioactive leak.
- On March 28, 1969, Greek poet and Nobel Prize laureate Giorgos Seferis publicly spoke out against the military junta in Greece on the BBC World Service.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)

- On March 28, 1556, the Fasli era, a historical calendar system, was introduced by Emperor Akbar.
- In 1737, the First Battle of Delhi took place, where the Marathas under Bajirao I defeated the Mughals.
- On March 28, 1942, Rashbehari Bose founded the Indian Independence League in Tokyo.
- In 1977, Morarji Desai formed a government in India.
- On March 28, 1992, J. R. D. Tata was conferred the Bharat Ratna, India's highest civilian honor.

## SECTION 25 — Strategic Insight / Underreported Development

A significant, yet underreported, development is the increasing recognition of "geoeconomic confrontation" as the top global risk for 2026, surpassing state-based armed conflict. This highlights a fundamental shift in global operating environments where economic tools like sanctions, trade restrictions, and supply chain re-organizations are increasingly used as strategic weapons, alongside traditional military actions. The fraying of transatlantic alliances and the intensified Great-Power competition between the US and China are undermining global stability, fostering an environment where new rules and norms for doing business are emerging, and old ones are being discarded. This trend suggests that economic resilience and adaptability will become even more critical for nations and corporations navigating a fragmented and contested multipolar world.
